tool_summary: Recursively searches directory trees and performs actions on matched files.

rules.description: Invokes an arbitrary command on each matched file via -exec. This allows execution of unvalidated code and can modify, delete, or exfiltrate files.
rules.reversible_note: Depends on the command executed — rm and mv are irreversible without backups; cat and ls are safe.
rules.notes: The {} placeholder is replaced by each found file path. Use + instead of \; to pass multiple paths as batch arguments to the command.

rules.description: Invokes a command from the directory of each matched file via -execdir. Higher risk than -exec due to potential path traversal or executing in the context of untrusted directories.
rules.reversible_note: Depends on the command executed — same considerations as -exec.
rules.notes: Like -exec but cd s into the file's directory before executing. This mitigates some symlink attacks but introduces directory-context risks where the working directory is untrusted.

rules.description: Deletes all matched files via -delete flag. No confirmation prompt; files are removed immediately.
Similar to -exec but prompts the user for confirmation before executing each command. Provides a safety margin but does not prevent the action if confirmed.
rules.reversible_note: Depends on the command executed and whether the user confirms each invocation.
rules.notes: Like -exec but presents a Y/n prompt per matched file. In non-interactive shells this will fail.
